documentation:
Function to compute a G statistic on a dataframe

Parameters
----------
df : pandas.DataFrame
    a pandas dataframe with a geometry column
cols : string or list of string
    name or list of names of columns to use to compute the statistic
w : pysal weights object
    a weights object aligned with the dataframe. If not provided, this
    is searched for in the dataframe's metadata
inplace : bool
    a boolean denoting whether to operate on the dataframe inplace or to
    return a series contaning the results of the computation. If
    operating inplace, the derived columns will be named 'column_g'
pvalue : string
    a string denoting which pvalue should be returned. Refer to the
    the G statistic's documentation for available p-values
outvals : list of strings
    list of arbitrary attributes to return as columns from the G statistic
**stat_kws : dict
    options to pass to the underlying statistic. For this, see the
    documentation for the G statistic.

Returns
--------
If inplace, None, and operation is conducted on dataframe
in memory. Otherwise, returns a copy of the dataframe with
the relevant columns attached.

Global G Autocorrelation Statistic

# Parameters

y

: Attribute values

w

: spatial weights instance as W or Graph aligned with y

permutations

: the number of random permutations for calculating pseudo p_values

# Attributes

y

: original variable

w

: spatial weights instance as W or Graph aligned with y

permutation

: the number of permutations

G

: the value of statistic

EG

: the expected value of statistic

VG

: the variance of G under normality assumption

z_norm

: standard normal test statistic

p_norm

: p-value under normality assumption (one-sided)

sim

: (if permutations > 0)
  vector of G values for permutated samples

p_sim

: p-value based on permutations (one-sided)
  null: spatial randomness
  alternative: the observed G is extreme it is either
  extremely high or extremely low

EG_sim

: average value of G from permutations

VG_sim

: variance of G from permutations

seG_sim

: standard deviation of G under permutations.

z_sim

: standardized G based on permutations

p_z_sim

: p-value based on standard normal approximation from permutations (one-sided)

# Notes

Moments are based on normality assumption.

For technical details see {cite}`Getis_2010` and {cite}`Ord_2010`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(10)
```

Preparing a point data set

```pycon
>>> points = [(10, 10), (20, 10), (40, 10), (15, 20), (30, 20), (30, 30)]
```

Creating a weights object from points

```pycon
>>> w = libpysal.weights.DistanceBand(points,threshold=15)
>>> w.transform = "B"
```

Preparing a variable

```pycon
>>> y = numpy.array([2, 3, 3.2, 5, 8, 7])
```

Applying Getis and Ord G test

```pycon
>>> from esda.getisord import G
>>> g = G(y,w)
```

Examining the results

```pycon
>>> round(g.G, 3)
0.557
```

```pycon
>>> round(g.p_norm, 3)
0.173
```

documentation:
Function to compute a G_Local statistic on a dataframe

Parameters
----------
df : pandas.DataFrame
    a pandas dataframe with a geometry column
cols : string or list of string
    name or list of names of columns to use to compute the statistic
w : W | Graph
    spatial weights instance as W or Graph aligned with the dataframe. If not
    provided, this is searched for in the dataframe's metadata
inplace : bool
    a boolean denoting whether to operate on the dataframe inplace or to
    return a series contaning the results of the computation. If
    operating inplace, the derived columns will be named 'column_g_local'
pvalue : string
    a string denoting which pvalue should be returned. Refer to the
    the G_Local statistic's documentation for available p-values
outvals : list of strings
    list of arbitrary attributes to return as columns from the
    G_Local statistic
**stat_kws : dict
    options to pass to the underlying statistic. For this, see the
    documentation for the G_Local statistic.

Returns
-------
pandas.DataFrame
                If inplace, None, and operation is conducted on dataframe
                in memory. Otherwise, returns a copy of the dataframe with
                the relevant columns attached.

Generalized Local G Autocorrelation

# Parameters

y

: variable

w

: spatial weights instance as W or Graph aligned with y

transform

: the type of w, either 'B' (binary) or 'R' (row-standardized)

permutations

: the number of random permutations for calculating
  pseudo p values

star

: whether or not to include focal observation in sums (default: False)
  if the row-transformed weight is provided, then this is the default
  value to use within the spatial lag. Generally, weights should be
  provided in binary form, and standardization/self-weighting will be
  handled by the function itself.

island_weight:

: value to use as a weight for the "fake" neighbor for every island.
  If numpy.nan, will propagate to the final local statistic depending
  on the `stat_func`. If 0, then the lag is always zero for islands.

# Attributes

y

: original variable

w

: spatial weights instance as W or Graph aligned with y

permutations

: the number of permutations

Gs

: of floats, the value of the orginal G statistic in Getis & Ord (1992)

EGs

: expected value of Gs under normality assumption
  the values is scalar, since the expectation is identical
  across all observations

VGs

: of floats, variance values of Gs under normality assumption

Zs

: of floats, standardized Gs

p_norm

: of floats, p-value under normality assumption (one-sided)
  for two-sided tests, this value should be multiplied by 2

sim

: of arrays of floats (if permutations>0), vector of I values
  for permutated samples

p_sim

: of floats, p-value based on permutations (one-sided)
  null - spatial randomness
  alternative - the observed G is extreme

  > (it is either extremely high or extremely low)

EG_sim

: of floats, average value of G from permutations

VG_sim

: of floats, variance of G from permutations

seG_sim

: of floats, standard deviation of G under permutations.

z_sim

: of floats, standardized G based on permutations

p_z_sim

: of floats, p-value based on standard normal approximation from
  permutations (one-sided)

# Notes

To compute moments of Gs under normality assumption,
PySAL considers w is either binary or row-standardized.
For binary weights object, the weight value for self is 1
For row-standardized weights object, the weight value for self is
1/(the number of its neighbors + 1).

For technical details see {cite}`Getis_2010` and {cite}`Ord_2010`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(10)
```

Preparing a point data set

```pycon
>>> points = [(10, 10), (20, 10), (40, 10), (15, 20), (30, 20), (30, 30)]
```

Creating a weights object from points

```pycon
>>> w = libpysal.weights.DistanceBand(points,threshold=15)
```

Preparing a variable

```pycon
>>> y = numpy.array([2, 3, 3.2, 5, 8, 7])
```

Applying Getis and Ord local G test using a binary weights object

```pycon
>>> from esda.getisord import G_Local
>>> lg = G_Local(y,w,transform='B')
```

Examining the results

```pycon
>>> lg.Zs
array([-1.0136729 , -0.04361589,  1.31558703, -0.31412676,  1.15373986,
        1.77833941])
>>> round(lg.p_sim[0], 3)
0.101
```

p-value based on standard normal approximation from permutations
\>>> round(lg.p_z_sim[0], 3)
0.154

```pycon
>>> numpy.random.seed(10)
```

Applying Getis and Ord local G\* test using a binary weights object

```pycon
>>> lg_star = G_Local(y,w,transform='B',star=True)
```

Examining the results

```pycon
>>> lg_star.Zs
array([-1.39727626, -0.28917762,  0.65064964, -0.28917762,  1.23452088,
        2.02424331])
>>> round(lg_star.p_sim[0], 3)
0.101
```

```pycon
>>> numpy.random.seed(12345)
```

Applying Getis and Ord local G test using a row-standardized weights object

```pycon
>>> lg = G_Local(y,w,transform='R')
```

Examining the results

```pycon
>>> lg.Zs
array([-0.62074534, -0.01780611,  1.31558703, -0.12824171,  0.28843496,
        1.77833941])
>>> round(lg.p_sim[0], 3)
0.103
```

```pycon
>>> numpy.random.seed(10)
```

Applying Getis and Ord local G\* test using a row-standardized weights object

```pycon
>>> lg_star = G_Local(y,w,transform='R',star=True)
```

Examining the results

```pycon
>>> lg_star.Zs
array([-0.62488094, -0.09144599,  0.41150696, -0.09144599,  0.24690418,
        1.28024388])
>>> round(lg_star.p_sim[0], 3)
0.101
```

```

Specifying the number of regions.

array([0.2375    , 0.26666667])
```

functions.name: esda.smoothing.direct_age_standardization
documentation:
A utility function to compute rate through direct age standardization

# Parameters

e

: (n\*h, 1), event variable measured for each age group across n spatial units

b

: (n\*h, 1), population at risk variable measured for each
  age group across n spatial units

s

: (n\*h, 1), standard population for each age group across n spatial units

n

: the number of spatial units

alpha

: significance level for confidence interval

# Notes

e, b, and s are arranged in the same order

# Returns

list

: a list of n tuples; a tuple has a rate and its lower and upper limits
  age standardized rates and confidence intervals

# Examples

Creating an array of an event variable (e.g., the number of cancer patients)
for 2 regions in each of which 4 age groups are available.
The first 4 values are event values for 4 age groups in the region 1,
and the next 4 values are for 4 age groups in the region 2.

```pycon
>>> e = np.array([30, 25, 25, 15, 33, 21, 30, 20])
```

Creating another array of a population-at-risk variable (e.g., total population)
for the same two regions.
The order for entering values is the same as the case of e.

```pycon
>>> b = np.array([1000, 1000, 1100, 900, 1000, 900, 1100, 900])
```

For direct age standardization, we also need the data for standard population.
Standard population is a reference population-at-risk (e.g., population
distribution for the U.S.) whose age distribution can be used as a benchmarking
point for comparing age distributions across regions (e.g., population
distribution for Arizona and California). Another array including standard
population is created.

```pycon
>>> s = np.array([1000, 900, 1000, 900, 1000, 900, 1000, 900])
```

Specifying the number of regions.

```pycon
>>> n = 2
```

Applying direct_age_standardization function to e and b

```pycon
>>> a, b = [i[0] for i in direct_age_standardization(e, b, s, n)]
>>> round(a, 4)
0.0237
>>> round(b, 4)
0.0267
```

functions.name: esda.smoothing.indirect_age_standardization
documentation:
A utility function to compute rate through indirect age standardization

# Parameters

e

: (n\*h, 1), event variable measured for each age group across n spatial units

b

: (n\*h, 1), population at risk variable measured for each age
  group across n spatial units

s_e

: (n\*h, 1), event variable measured for each age group across
  n spatial units in a standard population

s_b

: (n\*h, 1), population variable measured for each age group across
  n spatial units in a standard population

n

: the number of spatial units

alpha

: significance level for confidence interval

# Notes

e, b, s_e, and s_b are arranged in the same order

# Returns

list

: a list of n tuples; a tuple has a rate and its lower and upper limits
  age standardized rate

# Examples

Creating an array of an event variable (e.g., the number of cancer patients)
for 2 regions in each of which 4 age groups are available.
The first 4 values are event values for 4 age groups in the region 1,
and the next 4 values are for 4 age groups in the region 2.

```pycon
>>> e = np.array([30, 25, 25, 15, 33, 21, 30, 20])
```

Creating another array of a population-at-risk variable (e.g., total population)
for the same two regions.
The order for entering values is the same as the case of e.

```pycon
>>> b = np.array([100, 100, 110, 90, 100, 90, 110, 90])
```

For indirect age standardization, we also need the data for standard population
and event. Standard population is a reference population-at-risk (e.g.,
population distribution for the U.S.) whose age distribution can be used as a
benchmarking point for comparing age distributions across regions (e.g.,
popoulation distribution for Arizona and California). When the same concept is
applied to the event variable, we call it standard event (e.g., the number of
cancer patients in the U.S.). Two additional arrays including standard population
and event are created.

```pycon
>>> s_e = np.array([100, 45, 120, 100, 50, 30, 200, 80])
>>> s_b = np.array([1000, 900, 1000, 900, 1000, 900, 1000, 900])
```

Specifying the number of regions.

```pycon
>>> n = 2
```

Applying indirect_age_standardization function to e and b

```pycon
>>> [i[0] for i in indirect_age_standardization(e, b, s_e, s_b, n)]
[0.23723821989528798, 0.2610803324099723]
```

functions.name: esda.smoothing.standardized_mortality_ratio
documentation:
A utility function to compute standardized mortality ratio (SMR).

# Parameters

e

: (n\*h, 1), event variable measured for each age group across n spatial units

b

: (n\*h, 1), population at risk variable measured for each age
  group across n spatial units

s_e

: (n\*h, 1), event variable measured for each age group across
  n spatial units in a standard population

s_b

: (n\*h, 1), population variable measured for each age group
  across n spatial units in a standard population

n

: the number of spatial units

# Notes

e, b, s_e, and s_b are arranged in the same order

# Returns

array

: (nx1)

# Examples

Creating an array of an event variable (e.g., the number of cancer patients)
for 2 regions in each of which 4 age groups are available.
The first 4 values are event values for 4 age groups in the region 1,
and the next 4 values are for 4 age groups in the region 2.

```pycon
>>> e = np.array([30, 25, 25, 15, 33, 21, 30, 20])
```

Creating another array of a population-at-risk variable (e.g., total population)
for the same two regions.
The order for entering values is the same as the case of e.

```pycon
>>> b = np.array([100, 100, 110, 90, 100, 90, 110, 90])
```

To compute standardized mortality ratio (SMR),
we need two additional arrays for standard population and event.
Creating s_e and s_b for standard event and population, respectively.

```pycon
>>> s_e = np.array([100, 45, 120, 100, 50, 30, 200, 80])
>>> s_b = np.array([1000, 900, 1000, 900, 1000, 900, 1000, 900])
```

Specifying the number of regions.

```pycon
>>> n = 2
```

Applying indirect_age_standardization function to e and b

```pycon
>>> a, b = standardized_mortality_ratio(e, b, s_e, s_b, n)
>>> round(a, 4)
2.4869
>>> round(b, 4)
2.7368
```

methods.name: esda.smoothing._Smoother.by_col
documentation:
Compute smoothing by columns in a dataframe.

Parameters
-----------
df      :  pandas.DataFrame
           a dataframe containing the data to be smoothed
e       :  string or list of strings
           the name or names of columns containing event variables to be
           smoothed
b       :  string or list of strings
           the name or names of columns containing the population
           variables to be smoothed
inplace :  bool
           a flag denoting whether to output a copy of `df` with the
           relevant smoothed columns appended, or to append the columns
           directly to `df` itself.
**kwargs:  optional keyword arguments
           optional keyword options that are passed directly to the
           smoother.

Returns
---------
a copy of `df` containing the columns. Or, if `inplace`, this returns
None, but implicitly adds columns to `df`.

This is a helper class that implements things that all smoothers should do.
Right now, the only thing that we need to propagate is the by_col function.

TBQH, most of these smoothers should be functions, not classes (aside from
maybe headbanging triples), since they're literally only inits + one
attribute.

classes.name: esda.smoothing.Spatial_Filtering

methods.name: esda.smoothing.Spatial_Filtering.by_col
documentation:
Compute smoothing by columns in a dataframe. The bounding box and point
information is computed from the geometry column.

Parameters
-----------
df      :  pandas.DataFrame
           a dataframe containing the data to be smoothed
e       :  string or list of strings
           the name or names of columns containing event variables to be
           smoothed
b       :  string or list of strings
           the name or names of columns containing the population
           variables to be smoothed
x_grid  :  integer
           number of grid cells to use along the x-axis
y_grid  :  integer
           number of grid cells to use along the y-axis
geom_col:  string
           the name of the column in the dataframe containing the
           geometry information.
**kwargs:  optional keyword arguments
           optional keyword options that are passed directly to the
           smoother.
Returns
---------
a new dataframe of dimension (x_grid*y_grid, 3), containing the
coordinates of the grid cells and the rates associated with those grid
cells.

Spatial Filtering

# Parameters

bbox

: a bounding box for the entire n spatial units

data

: x, y coordinates

e

: event variable measured across n spatial units

b

: population at risk variable measured across n spatial units

x_grid

: the number of cells on x axis

y_grid

: the number of cells on y axis

r

: fixed radius of a moving window

pop

: population threshold to create adaptive moving windows

# Attributes

grid

: x, y coordinates for grid points

r

: rate values for grid points

# Notes

No tool is provided to find an optimal value for r or pop.

# Examples

Reading data in stl_hom.csv into stl to extract values
for event and population-at-risk variables

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> stl_ex = libpysal.examples.load_example('stl')
>>> stl = libpysal.io.open(stl_ex.get_path('stl_hom.csv'), 'r')
```

Reading the stl data in the WKT format so that
we can easily extract polygon centroids

```pycon
>>> from libpysal.io.util.wkt import WKTParser
>>> fromWKT = WKTParser()
>>> stl.cast('WKT',fromWKT)
```

Extracting polygon centroids through iteration

```pycon
>>> d = np.array([i.centroid for i in stl[:,0]])
```

Specifying the bounding box for the stl_hom data.
The bbox should includes two points for the left-bottom and the right-top corners

```pycon
>>> bbox = [[-92.700676, 36.881809], [-87.916573, 40.3295669]]
```

The 11th and 14th columns in stl_hom.csv includes the number
of homocides and population. Creating two arrays from these columns.

```pycon
>>> stl_e, stl_b = np.array(stl[:,10]), np.array(stl[:,13])
```

Applying spatial filtering by using a 10\*10 mesh grid and a moving window
with 2 radius

```pycon
>>> sf_0 = Spatial_Filtering(bbox,d,stl_e,stl_b,10,10,r=2)
```

Extracting the resulting rates through the property
r of the Spatial_Filtering instance

```pycon
>>> sf_0.r[:10]
array([4.23561763e-05, 4.45290850e-05, 4.56456221e-05, 4.49133384e-05,
       4.39671835e-05, 4.44903042e-05, 4.19845497e-05, 4.11936548e-05,
       3.93463504e-05, 4.04376345e-05])
```

Applying another spatial filtering by allowing the moving window to grow until
600000 people are found in the window

```pycon
>>> sf = Spatial_Filtering(bbox,d,stl_e,stl_b,10,10,pop=600000)
```

Checking the size of the reulting array including the rates

```pycon
>>> sf.r.shape
(100,)
```

Extracting the resulting rates through the property
r of the Spatial_Filtering instance

```pycon
>>> sf.r[:10]
array([3.73728738e-05, 4.04456300e-05, 4.04456300e-05, 3.81035327e-05,
       4.54831940e-05, 4.54831940e-05, 3.75658628e-05, 3.75658628e-05,
       3.75658628e-05, 3.75658628e-05])
```

classes.name: esda.smoothing.Headbanging_Triples
documentation:
Generate a pseudo spatial weights instance that contains headbanging triples

# Parameters

data

: numpy array of x, y coordinates

w : spatial weights instance
k : integer number of nearest neighbors
t : integer

> the number of triples

angle

: the angle criterium for a set of triples

edgecorr

: whether or not correction for edge points is made

# Attributes

triples

: key is observation record id, value is a list of lists of triple ids

extra

: key is observation record id, value is a list of the following:
  tuple of original triple observations
  distance between original triple observations
  distance between an original triple
  observation and its extrapolated point

# Examples

importing k-nearest neighbor weights creator

```pycon
>>> import libpysal # doctest: +SKIP
```

Reading data in stl_hom.csv into stl_db to extract values
for event and population-at-risk variables

```pycon
>>> stl_db = libpysal.io.open(
...     libpysal.examples.get_path('stl_hom.csv'),'r'
... ) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Reading the stl data in the WKT format so that
we can easily extract polygon centroids

```pycon
>>> from libpysal.io.util.wkt import WKTParser # doctest: +SKIP
>>> fromWKT = WKTParser() # doctest: +SKIP
>>> stl_db.cast('WKT',fromWKT) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Extracting polygon centroids through iteration

```pycon
>>> d = np.array([i.centroid for i in stl_db[:,0]]) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Using the centroids, we create a 5-nearst neighbor weights

```pycon
>>> w = libpysal.weights.KNN(d,k=5) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Ensuring that the elements in the spatial weights instance are ordered
by the order of stl_db's IDs

```pycon
>>> if not w.id_order_set: w.id_order = w.id_order # doctest: +SKIP
```

Finding headbaning triples by using 5 nearest neighbors

```pycon
>>> ht = Headbanging_Triples(d,w,k=5) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Checking the members of triples

```pycon
>>> for k, item in ht.triples.items()[:5]: print(k, item) # doctest: +SKIP
0 [(5, 6), (10, 6)]
1 [(4, 7), (4, 14), (9, 7)]
2 [(0, 8), (10, 3), (0, 6)]
3 [(4, 2), (2, 12), (8, 4)]
4 [(8, 1), (12, 1), (8, 9)]
```

Opening sids2.shp file

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> sids = libpysal.io.open(
...     libpysal.examples.get_path('sids2.shp'),'r'
... ) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Extracting the centroids of polygons in the sids data

```pycon
>>> sids_d = np.array([i.centroid for i in sids]) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Creating a 5-nearest neighbors weights from the sids centroids
\>>> sids_w = libpysal.weights.KNN(sids_d,k=5) # doctest: +SKIP

Ensuring that the members in sids_w are ordered by
the order of sids_d's ID

```pycon
>>> if not sids_w.id_order_set: sids_w.id_order = sids_w.id_order # doctest: +SKIP
```

Finding headbaning triples by using 5 nearest neighbors

```pycon
>>> s_ht = Headbanging_Triples(sids_d,sids_w,k=5) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Checking the members of the found triples

```pycon
>>> for k, item in s_ht.triples.items()[:5]: print(k, item) # doctest: +SKIP
0 [(1, 18), (1, 21), (1, 33)]
1 [(2, 40), (2, 22), (22, 40)]
2 [(39, 22), (1, 9), (39, 17)]
3 [(16, 6), (19, 6), (20, 6)]
4 [(5, 15), (27, 15), (35, 15)]
```

Finding headbanging triples by using 5 nearest neighbors with edge correction

```pycon
>>> s_ht2 = Headbanging_Triples(sids_d,sids_w,k=5,edgecor=True) # doctest: +SKIP
```

Checking the members of the found triples

```pycon
>>> for k, item in s_ht2.triples.items()[:5]: print(k, item) # doctest: +SKIP
0 [(1, 18), (1, 21), (1, 33)]
1 [(2, 40), (2, 22), (22, 40)]
2 [(39, 22), (1, 9), (39, 17)]
3 [(16, 6), (19, 6), (20, 6)]
4 [(5, 15), (27, 15), (35, 15)]
```

Checking the extrapolated point that is introduced into the triples
during edge correction

```pycon
>>> extrapolated = s_ht2.extra[72] # doctest: +SKIP
```

Checking the observation IDs constituting the extrapolated triple

```pycon
>>> extrapolated[0] # doctest: +SKIP
(89, 77)
```

Checking the distances between the extraploated point and the observation 89 and 77

```pycon
>>> round(extrapolated[1],5), round(extrapolated[2],6)  # doctest: +SKIP
(0.33753, 0.302707)
```

methods.name: esda.smoothing.Headbanging_Median_Rate.by_col
documentation:
Compute smoothing by columns in a dataframe. The bounding box and point
information is computed from the geometry column.

Parameters
-----------
df      :  pandas.DataFrame

           the name or names of columns containing event variables to be
